groups.name: Iconic regular hangout spots in TV shows
groups.explanation: The Drunken Clam (Family Guy), MacLaren's (How I Met Your Mother), The Peach Pit (Beverly Hills, 90210), and The Regal Beagle (Three's Company) are all the iconic regular hangout bar or social spot in their respective shows.

groups.name: Fictional TV towns that serve as a snooty or rival foil to the protagonist's hometown
groups.explanation: Stars Hollow (Gilmore Girls — the quaint protagonist town constantly set against the cold, elite world of Hartford and Chilton), Eagleton (Parks and Recreation — the wealthy, snooty rival town to Pawnee), Shelbyville (The Simpsons — the longstanding rival town to Springfield), and Stoneybrook (The Baby-Sitters Club — the affluent neighboring town serving as a foil to the protagonists' more grounded world) are all fictional TV towns defined narratively as a rival or contrasting counterpart to the main setting.

groups.name: TV creators who wrote for and starred in their own series
groups.explanation: Mindy Kaling (The Mindy Project), Lena Waithe (The Chi — creator and recurring star), Tina Fey (30 Rock — creator, head writer, and star), and Issa Rae (Insecure — creator, writer, and star) all created or co-created their signature TV shows, wrote for them, and starred as lead or major recurring characters in the same series.

groups.name: Female TV characters whose deaths were central to a male protagonist's story
groups.explanation: Rita Bennett (Dexter — her murder by the Trinity Killer is the inciting trauma that reshapes Dexter's arc), Lexi Reed (The Walking Dead — her sudden death devastates Daryl and reshapes his storyline), Zoe Barnes (House of Cards — pushed in front of a metro train by Frank Underwood, her death defines his ruthlessness), and Rosie Larsen (The Killing — her murder is the entire premise driving Detective Holder and the male figures in her life). All four are female characters whose deaths exist primarily to catalyze or define a male protagonist's journey, a classic example of the 'fridging' trope in prestige television.
